critic_raw:
The Hamiltonian Generative Network learns to integrate Hamiltonian dynamics directly from image sequences without domain knowledge. This involves simultaneously solving two problems: learning a suitable coordinate system in which the Hamiltonian is expressible, and learning the Hamiltonian function itself within that coordinate system. The paper's claim to work 'without restrictive domain assumptions' is undermined by the implicit assumption that the learned latent space will naturally factorize into canonical position-momentum pairs that satisfy the symplectic structure required for Hamiltonian mechanics. Nothing in the variational training objective enforces symplecticity — the model may learn a latent representation that is predictively useful without encoding the actual Hamiltonian structure, and rollouts that remain accurate over short horizons may accumulate energy errors that violate conservation laws over long horizons.

The evaluation systems (pendulum, spring-body, two/three body) are effectively integrable systems with low-dimensional phase spaces. These are the systems where Hamiltonian mechanics is most tractable and where structured priors (like Hamiltonian separability) are least needed. Testing on three physically similar systems with controlled initial conditions does not demonstrate that the approach generalizes to non-integrable or chaotic systems where the real difficulty of learning Hamiltonian structure lies.

The claim of both 'reconstruction and generative capabilities' encompasses two very different uses of the trained model. Reconstruction (predicting trajectory continuations for sequences seen in training) tests interpolation within the training distribution. Generation (sampling new trajectories) tests whether the learned dynamics are physically plausible for initial conditions not seen during training. These two evaluations require different metrics and are not interchangeable. If reconstruction error is reported as evidence for generative quality, the evaluation conflates them.

Rollout stability over long horizons is not reported. Variational models trained on short observation windows (as is typical for these systems) typically exhibit error accumulation when rolled out beyond the training horizon. If the paper only reports reconstruction accuracy on windows matching the training sequence length, the long-horizon rollout claim is unsupported.
all_issues_raised: The variational training objective does not enforce symplecticity in the latent space, so the model may learn predictively useful representations that do not encode genuine Hamiltonian structure, and energy conservation violations will manifest only at long rollout horizons not tested in the evaluation.

All evaluation systems (pendulum, spring-body, two/three body) are integrable with low-dimensional phase spaces — exactly the regime where the Hamiltonian assumption is least necessary; no non-integrable or high-dimensional systems are included to test the claimed generality.

Reconstruction and generation are evaluated as interchangeable capabilities, but reconstruction tests in-distribution interpolation while generation tests out-of-distribution physical plausibility — conflating them inflates generative performance estimates.
Long-horizon rollout stability is not verified; if evaluation windows match training sequence lengths, the rollout claim is untested and error accumulation over extended horizons is unknown.
all_issues_adjudicated: The variational training objective does not enforce symplecticity in the latent space, so the model may learn predictively useful representations that do not encode genuine Hamiltonian structure, and energy conservation violations will manifest only at long rollout horizons not tested in the evaluation.

All evaluation systems (pendulum, spring-body, two/three body) are integrable with low-dimensional phase spaces — exactly the regime where the Hamiltonian assumption is least necessary; no non-integrable or high-dimensional systems are included to test the claimed generality.

Reconstruction and generation are evaluated as interchangeable capabilities, but reconstruction tests in-distribution interpolation while generation tests out-of-distribution physical plausibility — conflating them inflates generative performance estimates.
Long-horizon rollout stability is not verified; if evaluation windows match training sequence lengths, the rollout claim is untested and error accumulation over extended horizons is unknown.
